title: Odisha's Jharsuguda hottest at 44.6°C; IMD issues heatwave, storm alerts across several states - CNBC TV18
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Odisha
full_text:
2 Min Read
India is set to witness a mix of intense heatwave conditions and scattered thunderstorms over the coming days, with the India Meteorological Department (IMD) cautioning against rising temperatures across several regions even as parts of the country brace for rain and gusty winds.
Odisha’s Jharsuguda recorded the highest maximum temperature in the country at 44.6°C in the 24 hours ending 8:30 am on April 23, the IMD said in its weather bulletin.
The IMD said heatwave conditions are “very likely” to persist in isolated pockets of northwest and central India over the next four to five days, and in eastern India over the next two to three days.
Regions including Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh, Bihar and parts of Uttar Pradesh are expected to remain under heatwave influence through April 27, while Delhi, Punjab, Haryana and Jharkhand may see such conditions on April 24 and 25.
Delhi-NCR to remain under heat stress
For Delhi-NCR, the IMD has forecast mainly clear skies on April 24, with maximum temperatures ranging between 42-44 degrees Celsius. Heatwave conditions are expected at isolated places, with temperatures remaining markedly above normal.
On April 25, similar conditions will continue, although there is a possibility of cloud formation and “thundery development towards afternoon/evening,” the IMD said.
By April 26, the sky is likely to turn partly cloudy, with a slight dip in temperatures, though they will still remain above normal.
Storms, rain to impact eastern and northeastern India
Even as northern and central parts face extreme heat, the IMD has forecast widespread rainfall and thunderstorm activity over the northeast and parts of eastern India through the week.
States such as Assam, Meghalaya, Arunachal Pradesh and the northeastern hill states are likely to witness fairly widespread rainfall accompanied by lightning and gusty winds. The IMD has also warned of isolated heavy to very heavy rainfall over these regions between April 25 and 29.
Eastern states including Bihar, Jharkhand, Odisha and West Bengal are also expected to receive scattered rainfall with thunderstorms and wind speeds reaching up to 50 kmph.
Thundersqualls, with gusts up to 70 kmph, are likely in parts of Bihar, Jharkhand and Sub-Himalayan West Bengal between April 25 and 28.

title: 44.6°C heatwave hits Jharsuguda, Odisha scorches under extreme summer - Dailyhunt
full_text:
: Odisha is reeling under an intense heatwave, with Jharsuguda emerging as the hottest place in the country after recording a scorching 44.6°C. Close behind is Talcher, where the temperature touched 43.5°C, reflecting the severity of the rising mercury levels across the state.
There seems to be no immediate relief from the sweltering heat, as the India Meteorological Department (IMD) has forecast that Odisha will continue to bake under extreme conditions for at least four more days. Temperatures in most districts have already crossed the 40°C mark, with over half of the state expected to suffer from severe heat and humidity until April 26.
The weather department has issued heatwave alerts for multiple districts, along with a yellow warning for hot and humid conditions in 23 districts. Additionally, 20 districts remain under yellow alert due to the ongoing harsh weather conditions.
IMD has further predicted that temperatures in Titilagarh, Bolangir, and Jharsuguda may soar up to 45°C in the coming days. While the heatwave is expected to persist until April 27, there is also a possibility of Kalbaisakhi (thunderstorms) in some regions, offering slight and temporary relief.
The relentless heat has significantly disrupted daily life. Streets are largely deserted during peak afternoon hours, as people avoid stepping out due to the unbearable sun and hot winds. The low humidity levels are worsening the situation, leading to excessive sweating and increasing the risk of heatstroke among residents.
Authorities have urged people to stay indoors during peak hours, stay hydrated, and take necessary precautions to avoid heat-related illnesses as the state battles one of the harshest summer spells of the season.
